after_id3 = [n for n in after_seg['nodes'] if n['id'] == 3][0]
print(f"\nID 3 before boundary: {before_id3['boundary']}
print(f"  after boundary: {after_id3['boundary']}

sections.content:
Now I can see what changed. Let me compare the key differences:
Before (ID 3 boundary changes:
- ID 3 (black shape): boundary changed - the shape moved or changed
- ID 4 (sky blue left): was [[49, 19], [49, 23], [53, 23], [53, 19]]
- After: [[49, 24], [49, 28], [53, 28], [53, 24]]
- ID 5 (sky blue right): was [[49, 39], [49, 43], [53, 43], [53, 39]]
- After: [[49, 34], [49, 38], [53, 38], [53, 34]]
So both sky blue squares moved right by 5 columns (19->24, 39->34). The black shape (ID 3) also changed shape.
Wait, let me look more carefully. The two sky blue squares moved from columns 19-23 and 39-43 to columns 24-28 and 34-38. That's a shift of +5 columns for the left one and -5 for the right one. They moved closer together.
Actually, looking at the boundaries more carefully:
- Left square: col 19-23 -> col 24-28 (shifted right by 5)
 Right square: col 39-43 -> col 34-38 (shifted left by 5)
So pressing RIGHT made the two figures move closer together (left one moves right, right one moves left).
This seems like the game is about making the game is about the two figures are two figures are moving toward each other. The goal might be to make them meet/collide.
Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see if they keep moving toward each other.
